nativeRange.habitat: Tropical Southeast Asian rivers and streams with rocky substrates, moderate to strong current, and mineral-rich hard, alkaline water. Brotia pagodula is restricted to the Thoungyin (Moei) River system on the Thailand-Myanmar border (a tributary of the Salween); Brotia herculea inhabits similar Thai river systems. Both species are found attached to rocks or on sandy bottoms in well-oxygenated water.

decorPreferences: sandy or fine smooth substrate (no sharp gravel that snags spiny shells)
smooth river rocks and rounded stones
low to moderate lighting (most Brotia prefer dim conditions)
avoid tight crevices that can trap spined Pagoda shells

diet.notes: Grazes biofilm, filamentous and diatom algae, and detritus. Will accept sinking algae wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, spinach), and occasional protein-based sinking foods. Calcium and mineral supplementation (cuttlebone, crushed coral in filter, or remineralised hard water) is important for shell integrity — these snails will erode and pit in soft water.

goodWith: peaceful community fish (small tetras, rasboras, danios)
other peaceful snails (Nerite, Mystery, Tylomelania rabbit snails)
Sulawesi tankmates given the warm, hard, alkaline parameter overlap
avoidWith: snail-eating fish (pufferfish, loaches such as clown/yoyo)

summary: Umbrella entry for the Brotia trade genus — tropical Southeast Asian (mostly Thai) livebearing snails including the large ridged-shell Hercules (B. herculea) and the spiny pagoda-shaped Pagoda Snail (B. pagodula). Specialty-importer species requiring hard, alkaline water.
careNotes: This entry covers the Brotia trade genus as imported from Southeast Asia, primarily two species: Brotia herculea (the Hercules or White Hercules Snail — a large snail with a heavy, ridged conical shell, often white, cream, or with purple stripes; reaches roughly 7–10 cm) and Brotia pagodula (the Pagoda or Horned Armor Snail — smaller at roughly 3–5 cm but with a striking stepped, pagoda-shaped shell bearing sharp spiny protrusions). Other Brotia spp. occasionally appear in the trade. Both species are sourced almost exclusively from specialty importers such as Aqua Imports rather than mainstream retail. Care is broadly similar to Tylomelania rabbit snails: warm (22–28 °C), neutral to alkaline (pH 7.0–8.0), and hard water (GH 8–20, KH 5–15) with adequate calcium and minerals. Sources disagree mildly on temperature and pH ceilings — Aqua Imports lists 72–78 °F / pH 6.5–7.5 for the Hercules, while Aquatic Arts and AquariumBreeder report a broader 22–28 °C / pH 7.0–8.2; the file uses the averaged middle range. The lower acceptable pH is debated, but a slightly alkaline tank with stable mineral content is the more reliable target — soft, acidic water tends to erode and pit the shells. Sources also disagree on plant safety: Aqua Imports describes both species as 'not plant safe', AquariumBreeder notes Pagoda Snails will consume live plants if underfed, and Aquatic Arts considers the Hercules generally plant-safe; the practical guidance is to consider them at-risk for delicate plants and to keep them well-fed if planted. A sandy or fine smooth substrate is preferred; sharp gravel can snag the spiny Pagoda shell, and tight crevices can trap them. Brotia are sensitive to poor water quality (ammonia, nitrite, accumulated nitrate) and to copper-based medications and fertilisers. Acclimation should be slow; sudden parameter swings cause stress and shell damage. A 40 L (10 gal) tank is a reasonable floor per Aqua Imports, with 75 L or larger giving better long-term stability for groups. Tank size: sources span 10 gal (38 L) to 20 gal (76 L) minimums — 40 L picked as the cautious nice round value at the lower end of consensus.
breedingNotes: Like other Pachychilidae, Brotia species are ovoviviparous livebearers — females retain developing embryos in a brood pouch and release fully-formed miniature snails rather than laying egg clutches. Reproduction in captivity is generally slow: AquariumBreeder reports roughly 10–20 juveniles per year for B. pagodula, typically released 1–3 at a time, with breeding rated difficult and a recommended starting group of 4–5 individuals to ensure both sexes are present. Sexes are not externally distinguishable. Stable hard alkaline water, abundant biofilm, and the absence of snail predators (assassin snails, loaches, pufferfish) are required for juveniles to survive. There is no risk of explosive population growth as with bladder or ramshorn snails.

primary.name: Aqua Imports
primary.notes: Specialty importer product page for the White Hercules Snail (Brotia herculea). Source for primary identification of the trade name, native range (Wild Thailand), conservative water parameter targets (72–78 °F, pH 6.5–7.5), 3-inch adult size, 10-gallon minimum tank, omnivorous-grazer diet, and 'not plant-safe' caution.

additional.name: Aqua Imports — Pagoda Snail
additional.notes: Designated secondary; covers the sibling species Brotia pagodula sold under this umbrella entry. Confirms Thai (Thoungyin River) provenance, 72–78 °F / pH 7.0–8.0 / advice that higher KH and GH aid shell integrity, 2-inch adult size, 10-gallon minimum tank, peaceful grazer temperament, and 'not plant safe' caution.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder — Brotia pagodula Detailed Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature 22–26 °C (optimal 24–25 °C), pH 7.0–8.0, GH 6–20, KH 5–15, 3–5 cm adult size, 1–2 yr captive lifespan, 10 gal / ~40 L minimum tank, ovoviviparous (livebearing) reproduction with ~10–20 juveniles/yr, breeding rated difficult, family Pachychilidae, copper sensitivity, and that plants may be eaten if food is insufficient.

additional.name: Aquatic Arts — Hercules Snail (Brotia herculea)
additional.notes: Cross-checked Brotia herculea temperature 22–28 °C, pH 7.0–8.2, 3–4+ inch adult size, 20-gal minimum tank, peaceful temperament, generally shrimp- and plant-safe (in contrast with Aqua Imports' 'not plant safe' caution — the file resolves this as 'with caution'), livebearing reproduction, and copper sensitivity.
